result:
The first time I realized the sky was a kind of soft, bruised ceiling, I was lying on the trampoline in the backyard my father sold the next year to pay the tax man. I was nine, maybe ten, and I had been counting shooting stars like they were the beads on my mother’s broken rosary. The trampoline had a slow leak; every bounce was an argument with gravity. I remember thinking: if I stop moving, the earth will remember I’m here and come to collect. So I kept jumping, small, apologetic bounces, until the stars themselves looked like punctuation marks in a sentence too long to read aloud.

I used to keep a notebook of lost things: one eyeglass lens, spearmint gum wrappers, the sound of the upstairs toilet flushing at 2:14 a.m., my virginity, the smell of my grandfather’s garage (metal shavings and Nescafé). I numbered them like specimens. I was convinced that if I wrote them down, they’d have to come back, the way missing socks are rumored to return if you simply leave the dryer door open and sing the jingle from the commercial of the brand that manufactured them. None of them ever did. Instead, the notebook grew pregnant with absence; it got fat on hunger. One winter I used it to prop up the short leg of the kitchen table; my mother never noticed, but the salt shaker rolled toward me every dinner like it wanted to confess.

There is a word—I found it years later in a book I stole from a hostel in Lisbon—that means “the sadness of not having anyone miss you while you’re still alive.” The word is shaped like a throat clearing. I tried to pronounce it to a bartender who claimed he could speak nine languages; he poured me a ghost of absinthe and said, “That isn’t sorrow, that’s just Thursday.” I wrote the word on the inside of my forearm with eyeliner so I wouldn’t forget, but sweat blurred it into a bruise that looked like my childhood sky. When I washed it off, the skin underneath was lighter, a reverse shadow. I thought: maybe this is how we heal, by rubbing away the syllables that name our pain.

I keep misplacing my adult self. I leave it in rideshares, airplane seat pockets, the tiny paper envelopes the dentist fills with fluoride. I have a theory that I’m actually eight different people who take shifts. The night shift me smokes clove cigarettes even though I hate them; the morning shift me alphabetizes the spice rack and tweets about neurodivergence. Between shifts there is a moment, like the hush between lightning and thunder, where nobody is driving, and that’s when the body does what it wants—buys a one-way ticket to Reykjavík, kisses a stranger who tastes of ash and cardamom, writes unsendable emails to the dead. Once I found a Polaroid in my coat pocket of a woman who might be me asleep on a beach. On the back someone wrote, in handwriting I almost recognize, “You were heavier in the dream.”

Memory smells like the inside of a cast iron skillet left to soak too long: metallic, ancestral, a little like blood. My memories have started multiplying like fruit flies; they hover in clouds over the compost bin of last summer. I swat them, but they only divide. There is now an entire battalion of the moment my father taught me to tie my shoes—one loop, swoop, pull, the rabbit running around the tree and into the hole—and they all play at once, a looping gif of learning how to stay tethered to the world. I try to step inside one of them, to touch the stiff denim of his jeans, to smell the Listerine on his breath, but I bounce off like the trampoline. The memory won’t let me edit the ending where he walks away. It insists on fidelity to the first draft.

Sometimes I think the universe is an only child making up friends out of debris. It scatters them across black construction paper, spins them so fast they look permanent. We’re the glitter that sticks to its fingers after the art project; we cling, we shimmer, we get washed down the drain when the science fair ends. I told this to a physicist on a dating app who unmatched me after I said I didn’t believe in dark matter, only dark humor. Still, I keep swiping, because somewhere there must be someone who understands that loneliness is just gravity looking for a friend.

Yesterday I found a voicemail from 2013 on an old phone. It was my grandmother singing “Happy Birthday” in Polish, off-key, her voice cracking like thin ice. Halfway through she forgets my name and calls me by my father’s. I listened to it six times, then made a pear upside-down cake using her recipe, substituting thyme for rosemary because grief needs a plot twist. The cake stuck to the pan; I spooned it out anyway, a lumpy constellation of caramel and regret. I ate two pieces standing over the sink. The third piece I left on the windowsill for the raven that sometimes taps the glass. He took it, left a single black feather and a pebble shaped like a molar. I put them in the notebook, entry #1004: evidence that someone was here.

At night I practice dying, just to stay limber. I close my eyes and flatten my breathing until it resembles the shoreline of a lake so still it reflects the moon like a coin pressed to glass. I imagine my name evaporating from group chats, my profile photos bleaching into ghost stains, my books going out of print. I watch the timeline scroll past without me, a conveyor belt of hot takes and newborn babies that never pauses. It feels like taking off a backpack I didn’t know I was wearing; the straps leave grooves that ache sweetly. Then my heart hiccups, afraid of getting what it rehearsed, and I snap the lights on, gasping like a fish benched mid-game. The ceiling fan wobbles overhead, doing its impression of a tired helicopter. I tell it, “Not tonight.” I tell the dark, “I still have groceries to use up.” I tell the notebook, “One more page.”

I have 1,000 words, but the spaces between them are where I actually live. In the pauses, I keep a spare key to the trampoline, a thimble of my father’s aftershave, the hush before the match ignites. If you listen closely, you can hear the sky inflating, slow as a lung learning to breathe outside the body. I’m learning to breathe there, too—upside-down, inside-out, cheek against the cool underbelly of the world. It turns out the sky is not a ceiling but a trampoline, and we are all of us jumping, small, apologetic bounces, hoping the stars are keeping score, hoping gravity forgets our names, hoping the next jump flings us high enough to glimpse the notebook’s last page, blank and dazzling, ready for whatever we haven’t lost yet.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
